parts.content: Use **Dijkstra's Algorithm** to find the shortest path from vertex $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{E}$. Show all steps.
parts.markscheme:
- Initialize the vertex distances: $\mathrm{A} = 0$ and all other vertices to $\infty$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Update distances by processing nodes in order of shortest distance:
  - From $\mathrm{A}$: update $\mathrm{B} = 4$, $\mathrm{C} = 7$
  - From $\mathrm{B}$: update $\mathrm{D} = 7$, $\mathrm{C} = 6$
  - From $\mathrm{C}$: update $\mathrm{E} = 7$
  - From $\mathrm{D}$: no shorter path found to $\mathrm{E}$ (distance remains $7$) <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final shortest path: $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{E}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the total shortest distance: $4 + 2 + 1 = 7$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Initialize the Algorithm
steps.content:
To find the shortest path from vertex $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{E}$ using **Dijkstra's Algorithm**, we begin by setting the distance to our starting node, $\mathrm{A}$, to $0$ and all other nodes to infinity ($\infty$). 

We will track these distances in a table. In each step, we select the unvisited vertex with the smallest distance, mark it as 'visited', and update the distances to its neighbors.

highlights.text: Initialize the vertex distances: \mathrm{A} = 0 and all other vertices to \infty

steps.title: Set Up the Table
steps.content:
Our initial table looks like this:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Vertex Visited} & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline \text{Initial State} & 0 & \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \hline \end{array}$$

steps.title: Visit Vertex A
steps.content:
We start at **A**. Looking at the graph, we can reach:
- **B** with a weight of $4$. New distance: $0 + 4 = 4$.
- **C** with a weight of $7$. New distance: $0 + 7 = 7$.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Vertex Visited} & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline \text{Initial State} & 0 & \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \hline A & (0) & 4 & 7 & \infty & \infty \\ \hline \end{array}$$
(Brackets indicate a vertex is officially visited).

gdc.instructions: Simple addition.

ti84.instructions: Simple addition for edge weights.

desmos.instructions: Add the starting distance 0 to the edge weights.

calculator.description: Calculate distances from A

steps.title: Visit Vertex B
steps.content:
The smallest unvisited distance is $4$ at **B**. From **B**, we can reach:
- **C**: $4 + 2 = 6$. Since $6 < 7$, we update C's distance.
- **D**: $4 + 3 = 7$. 

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Vertex Visited} & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline A & (0) & 4 & 7 & \infty & \infty \\ \hline B & (0) & (4) & 6 & 7 & \infty \\ \hline \end{array}$$

gdc.instructions: Add B's distance to weights.

ti84.instructions: Add B's current distance (4) to the edge weights to C (2) and D (3).

desmos.instructions: Sum the current shortest path to B with its neighbors' edge weights.

calculator.description: Calculate updated distances from B

steps.title: Visit Vertex C
steps.content:
The smallest unvisited distance is now $6$ at **C**. From **C**, we can reach:
- **E**: $6 + 1 = 7$.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Vertex Visited} & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline B & (0) & (4) & 6 & 7 & \infty \\ \hline C & (0) & (4) & (6) & 7 & 7 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

ti84.instructions: Add C's distance (6) to the edge weight to E (1).

calculator.description: Calculate distance to E via C

steps.title: Visit Remaining Vertices
steps.content:
Both **D** and **E** have a distance of $7$. If we visit **D**, we check if it offers a shorter path to **E**, but there is no shorter connection found. Finally, we visit **E**.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Vertex Visited} & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline C & (0) & (4) & (6) & 7 & 7 \\ \hline D & (0) & (4) & (6) & (7) & 7 \\ \hline E & (0) & (4) & (6) & (7) & (7) \\ \hline \end{array}$$

highlights.text: From \mathrm{D}: no shorter path found to \mathrm{E} (distance remains 7)

steps.title: Identify the Shortest Path
steps.content:
By tracing back the updates, we can see how we arrived at the value of $7$ for vertex $\mathrm{E}$:
- $\mathrm{E}$ came from $\mathrm{C}$ ($6+1$)
- $\mathrm{C}$ came from $\mathrm{B}$ ($4+2$)
- $\mathrm{B}$ came from $\mathrm{A}$ ($0+4$)

The shortest path is **A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ E** with a total distance of **7**.

steps.title: Set up the labels
steps.content:
To use the **Graphical Labeling (Box Method)**, we assign a pair of numbers to each vertex: a **temporary label** (the current shortest distance found) and a **permanent label** (once we are certain it is the shortest possible distance).

We start at vertex $\mathrm{A}$. Since it's our starting point, its distance is $0$. We mark this as permanent using a box: **$[0]$**.

All other vertices begin with a temporary distance of $\infty$.

highlights.text: Initialize the vertex distances: A = 0 and all other vertices to ∞

steps.title: Update from Vertex A
steps.content:
From the permanent vertex $\mathrm{A}[0]$, we look at its neighbors, $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$:

- Distance to $\mathrm{B}$: $0 + 4 = 4$
- Distance to $\mathrm{C}$: $0 + 7 = 7$

We write these as temporary labels next to the vertices. Between $4$ and $7$, $4$ is the smallest, so we make the label at $\mathrm{B}$ permanent: **$[4]$**.

steps.title: Update from Vertex B
steps.content:
Now we look at the neighbors of our new permanent vertex $\mathrm{B}[4]$:

- **Vertex $\mathrm{C}$**: The current temporary label is $7$. From $\mathrm{B}$, the distance is $4 + 2 = 6$. Since $6 < 7$, we update the temporary label at $\mathrm{C}$ to $6$.
- **Vertex $\mathrm{D}$**: From $\mathrm{B}$, the distance is $4 + 3 = 7$. We assign a temporary label of $7$ to $\mathrm{D}$.

The smallest temporary label is now at $\mathrm{C}$ ($6$), so we make it permanent: **$[6]$**.

steps.title: Update from Vertex C
steps.content:
Next, we look at the neighbors of $\mathrm{C}[6]$:

- **Vertex $\mathrm{E}$**: From $\mathrm{C}$, the distance is $6 + 1 = 7$. We assign a temporary label of $7$.
- **Vertex $\mathrm{D}$**: From $\mathrm{C}$, the distance is $6 + 1 = 7$. The current label is already $7$, so there is no change.

Both $\mathrm{D}$ and $\mathrm{E}$ have a temporary label of $7$. We can now make $\mathrm{E}$ permanent: **$[7]$**.

steps.title: Trace the shortest path
steps.content:
To find the path, we work backwards from $\mathrm{E}$ to $\mathrm{A}$ by checking which edge weight matches the difference between permanent labels:

- $\mathrm{E}[7] - \mathrm{C}[6] = 1$ (This matches edge $\mathrm{CE}$)
- $\mathrm{C}[6] - \mathrm{B}[4] = 2$ (This matches edge $\mathrm{BC}$)
- $\mathrm{B}[4] - \mathrm{A}[0] = 4$ (This matches edge $\mathrm{AB}$)

The shortest path is **$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{E}$** with a total distance of **$7$**.

gdc.instructions: Type 4 + 2 + 1 and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 2 + 1 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input 4 + 2 + 1 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Verify the sum of the edge weights for the identified path.

highlights.text: State the final shortest path: A → B → C → E

steps.title: Define the Eulerian condition
steps.content:
To determine if a graph is **Eulerian**, we check the degree of every vertex. For a connected graph to be Eulerian, **every single vertex must have an even degree**.

In an adjacency matrix, we can find the degree of a vertex by using the **Row Summation Method**: simply add up all the entries in that vertex's row.

steps.title: Calculate vertex degrees
steps.content:
Let's calculate the sum for each row of the matrix $A$ to find the degree of each of the 4 vertices:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Degree of } V_1 &= 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \\ \text{Degree of } V_2 &= 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\ \text{Degree of } V_3 &= 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 3 \\ \text{Degree of } V_4 &= 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \end{aligned}$$

The set of degrees for this graph is $\{2, 3, 3, 2\}$.

steps.title: Analyze the results
steps.content:
Now we look at our results: $\{2, 3, 3, 2\}$. 

We can see that **Vertex 2** and **Vertex 3** both have a degree of **3**, which is an **odd number**. 

Since the Eulerian condition requires *all* vertices to have an even degree, having even one odd degree is enough to disqualify the graph.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Because not all vertices have an even degree (specifically, there are two vertices with an odd degree), the graph is **not Eulerian**.

This is a standard requirement covered in **AHL 3.14: Graph theory**. Remember: 
- All even degrees = Eulerian graph.
- Exactly two odd degrees = Semi-Eulerian graph (has an Eulerian trail but not a circuit).
- More than two odd degrees = Neither.

steps.title: Interpret the adjacency matrix
steps.content:
To visualize the graph, we look at the connections defined by the adjacency matrix $A$. Each row represents a vertex, and the '1's show which other vertices it is connected to. 

Let the vertices be $V_1, V_2, V_3,$ and $V_4$. From the matrix:
- $V_1$ is connected to $V_2$ and $V_3$.
- $V_2$ is connected to $V_1, V_3,$ and $V_4$.
- $V_3$ is connected to $V_1, V_2,$ and $V_4$.
- $V_4$ is connected to $V_2$ and $V_3$.

steps.title: Determine vertex degrees
steps.content:
In a graph, the **degree** of a vertex is the number of edges connected to it. Visually, this is the number of lines meeting at a point. We can find this by summing the entries in each row of the adjacency matrix:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{deg}(V_1) &= 0+1+1+0 = 2 \\ \text{deg}(V_2) &= 1+0+1+1 = 3 \\ \text{deg}(V_3) &= 1+1+0+1 = 3 \\ \text{deg}(V_4) &= 0+1+1+0 = 2 \end{aligned}$$

The degrees of the vertices are $\{2, 3, 3, 2\}$.

highlights.text: Determine the degree of each vertex by summing the entries in each row: {2, 3, 3, 2}

steps.title: Apply the Eulerian condition
steps.content:
For a connected graph to be **Eulerian** (meaning it contains an Eulerian circuit), **every vertex must have an even degree**.

Looking at our degrees:
- $V_1$ and $V_4$ have even degrees ($2$).
- $V_2$ and $V_3$ have **odd** degrees ($3$).

Because there are vertices with odd degrees, the graph does not satisfy the condition for being Eulerian.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: The graph is **not Eulerian** because not all vertices have an even degree (specifically, there are exactly two vertices with a degree of 3).

highlights.text: Conclude that the graph is not Eulerian because not all vertices have an even degree (or there are exactly two odd-degree vertices)

steps.title: Understand the Chinese Postman Problem
steps.content:
The Chinese Postman Problem (also known as the Route Inspection Problem) asks for the shortest path that traverses every edge of a graph at least once and returns to the starting point. 

If all vertices have an even degree (the number of edges connected to them), the graph is **Eulerian**, and the total distance is simply the sum of all edge weights. If there are odd vertices, we must use the **Standard Route Inspection Algorithm** to find which edges need to be repeated.

steps.title: Identify odd vertices
steps.content:
First, we examine the degree of each vertex in the graph. The degree is the number of edges meeting at that vertex. By checking the given graph, we find that most vertices are even, but two are odd:

*   **Vertex $B$** and **Vertex $F$** are the only vertices with an odd degree.

In any graph, there will always be an even number of odd vertices. Since we only have two, we know we will need to repeat a path between them.

highlights.text: Identify the odd vertices: B and F

steps.title: Find the shortest connection
steps.content:
To minimize the total distance, we must find the shortest path between our two odd vertices, $B$ and $F$. 

Looking at the graph, the shortest path from $B$ to $F$ goes through vertex $D$:

$$\text{Path } BDF = BD + DF = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9$$

This $0.9$ represents the extra distance we must travel to ensure every edge is covered.

highlights.text: State the shortest connection between odd vertices: BDF = 0.9

steps.title: Sum all edge weights
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the sum of every edge weight in the original graph. This represents the distance if we traveled every edge exactly once.

$$\text{Sum} = 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.9 + 0.8 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.6 = 7.8$$

gdc.instructions: Add all individual edge weights.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of weights on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the sum of all weights into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of all edge weights.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Finally, to solve the Chinese Postman Problem, we add the shortest connection between the odd vertices to the sum of all edges:

$$\text{Total distance} = \text{Sum of all edges} + \text{Shortest connection between odd vertices}$$

$$\text{Total distance} = 7.8 + 0.9 = 8.7$$

Thus, the minimum distance required to traverse every edge and return to the start is $8.7$.

steps.title: Understand the Chinese Postman Problem
steps.content:
The Chinese Postman Problem (also known as the Route Inspection Problem) asks us to find the shortest possible route that visits every edge of a graph at least once and returns to the starting point.

To do this efficiently, we want to find an **Eulerian circuit**. A graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if every vertex has an **even degree** (an even number of edges connected to it). If a graph has odd vertices, we must repeat some edges to make all vertices even.

steps.title: Identify the odd vertices
steps.content:
First, we look at the degree of each vertex in the graph to find which ones are odd:

- Vertex **$B$**: Connected to $A$, $C$, and $D$ (Degree 3) $\rightarrow$ **Odd**
- Vertex **$F$**: Connected to $D$, $G$, and $I$ (Degree 3) $\rightarrow$ **Odd**

All other vertices have even degrees (e.g., $A$ is 2, $D$ is 4, $G$ is 4). Thus, the odd vertices are **$B$** and **$F$**.

steps.title: Find the shortest connection
steps.content:
Since $B$ and $F$ are the only two odd vertices, we need to find the shortest path between them to repeat (augment). This will effectively add one to the degree of $B$ and $F$, making them even.

Looking at the graph, the path **$B \to D \to F$** is the most direct:
- Edge **$BD$** = $0.4$
- Edge **$DF$** = $0.5$

Total distance for path $BDF$:

$$0.4 + 0.5 = 0.9$$

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
To solve the problem, we take the sum of all existing edge weights and add the weight of the shortest path we found to repeat.

From the data provided, the sum of all original edge weights is:

$$0.2+0.3+0.2+0.5+0.5+0.6+0.6+0.5+0.4+0.5+0.4+0.9+0.8+0.4+0.4+0.6 = 7.8$$

Now, we add the repeated path $BDF$ ($0.9$):

$$7.8 + 0.9 = 8.7$$

gdc.instructions: Add the total weight of the graph to the weight of the shortest path between odd vertices.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the original edges followed by the shortest repeated path.

desmos.instructions: Simply perform the addition in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total distance for the Chinese Postman Problem.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The total distance for the Chinese postman problem is **$8.7$**.

In an exam, you earn marks for:
1. Correctly identifying vertices $B$ and $F$ as odd.
2. Finding the shortest distance between them ($0.9$).
3. Adding this to the total weight of the graph.

parts.content: Explain why every connected graph with $v$ vertices and $v$ or more edges must contain at least one cycle.
parts.markscheme:
A tree with $v$ vertices has $v - 1$ edges <marks>M1</marks>

So if a connected graph has $v$ or more edges, it has more edges than a tree and must therefore contain at least one cycle <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define a tree
steps.content:
To understand why a graph must have a cycle, we first look at the simplest way to connect vertices: a **tree**. 

By definition, a tree is a connected graph that contains **no cycles**. It is the most efficient way to link a set of vertices together.

steps.title: State tree edge property
steps.content:
In **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (AHL 3.14)**, we learn a fundamental property of trees: a tree with $v$ vertices must have exactly $v - 1$ edges.

$$e = v - 1$$

This is the maximum number of edges a connected graph can have before a cycle is inevitably created.

steps.title: Compare edges and conclude
steps.content:
We are looking at a graph that has $v$ or more edges. Since $v$ is greater than $v - 1$, this graph has more edges than a tree with the same number of vertices.

Because a tree is the "largest" connected graph possible without any cycles, adding that extra edge (bringing the total to $v$ or more) must close a path and form at least one cycle.

highlights.text: every connected graph with $v$ vertices and $v$ or more edges must contain at least one cycle

steps.title: Define a Spanning Tree
steps.content:
To solve this using the spanning tree argument, we first recall that every connected graph with $v$ vertices contains at least one **spanning tree**. 

A spanning tree is a subgraph that includes all $v$ vertices of the original graph but has no cycles. It is the "skeleton" that keeps the graph connected using the minimum number of edges possible.

steps.title: Identify the Edge Requirement
steps.content:
A fundamental property of any tree (including a spanning tree) is the relationship between its vertices and edges.

For a tree with $v$ vertices, the number of edges is exactly:

$$e = v - 1$$

highlights.text: A tree with v vertices has v - 1 edges

steps.title: Analyze Adding Extra Edges
steps.content:
If a connected graph has $v$ edges, it has one more edge than its spanning tree. 

When you add any additional edge to a tree, you are connecting two vertices that are already connected by a path within that tree. This action necessarily completes a circuit, creating at least one **cycle**.

steps.title: Conclusion for $v$ Edges
steps.content: Since $v > v - 1$, any connected graph with $v$ or more edges contains more edges than its own spanning tree. Therefore, it must contain at least one cycle to accommodate those extra edges while remaining connected.

highlights.text: So if a connected graph has v or more edges, it has more edges than a tree and must therefore contain at least one cycle

steps.title: State Euler's Formula
steps.content:
For any connected planar graph, Euler's formula relates the number of vertices ($v$), edges ($e$), and faces ($f$):

$$v - e + f = 2$$

Note that the number of faces ($f$) includes the single infinite exterior region.

steps.title: Rearrange for faces
steps.content:
To determine the number of faces based on the number of edges and vertices, we rearrange the formula to solve for $f$:

$$f = e - v + 2$$

steps.title: Apply the given condition
steps.content:
The question specifies that the graph has $v$ or more edges. We can express this as the inequality:

$$e \geq v$$

If we subtract $v$ from both sides, we get:

$$e - v \geq 0$$

steps.title: Determine number of faces
steps.content:
Now, substitute the inequality $(e - v) \geq 0$ back into our rearranged Euler's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= (e - v) + 2 \\
f &\geq 0 + 2 \\
f &\geq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the graph must have at least 2 faces.

steps.title: Relate faces to cycles
steps.content:
In graph theory, a graph with no cycles (a tree) has only **one** face: the unbounded exterior region. 

Since we have shown that $f \geq 2$, there must be at least one additional face. For an additional face to exist, a set of edges must enclose an area, which defines the existence of at least one cycle.

parts.content: Create the adjacency matrix.

steps.title: Define the matrix structure
steps.content:
An adjacency matrix for a graph with $n$ vertices is an $n \times n$ square matrix. Since our graph has 4 vertices (1, 2, 3, and 4), we will create a $4 \times 4$ matrix. 

In this matrix, the rows represent the **starting vertex** and the columns represent the **ending vertex**. We enter a 1 if a directed edge (an arrow) exists from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$, and a 0 otherwise.

steps.title: Analyze Row 1 (Vertex 1)
steps.content:
First, we look at **Vertex 1** to see where its outgoing arrows point:
- There is an edge from 1 to 2.
- There are no edges from 1 to itself, 3, or 4.

Row 1 becomes:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: Analyze Row 2 (Vertex 2)
steps.content:
Next, we look at **Vertex 2**:
- There is an edge from 2 to 1.
- There is an edge from 2 to 3.
- There are no edges from 2 to itself or 4.

Row 2 becomes:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: Analyze Row 3 (Vertex 3)
steps.content:
Now we look at **Vertex 3**:
- By observing the graph, we can see that Vertex 3 has no outgoing arrows at all. 

Row 3 becomes:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: Analyze Row 4 (Vertex 4)
steps.content:
Finally, we look at **Vertex 4**:
- There is an edge from 4 to 1.
- There is an edge from 4 to 2.
- There are no edges from 4 to itself or 3.

Row 4 becomes:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: Construct the final matrix
steps.content:
By combining our four rows in order, we get the complete adjacency matrix:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.title: Identify the vertices
steps.content: First, we observe that the graph has 4 vertices: $V_{1}, V_{2}, V_{3},$ and $V_{4}$. Since there are 4 vertices, our adjacency matrix will be a $4 \times 4$ matrix. In this matrix, the rows represent the starting vertex and the columns represent the ending vertex.

steps.title: List directed edges
steps.content:
Using the **Edge Listing** part of our method, we look at the arrows in the diagram to identify all directed edges. We list them as coordinate pairs $(start, end)$:

*   From $V_{1}$: there is one arrow pointing to $V_{2}$. $\to (1, 2)$
*   From $V_{2}$: there are arrows pointing to $V_{1}$ and $V_{3}$. $\to (2, 1)$ and $(2, 3)$
*   From $V_{3}$: there are no arrows leaving this vertex.
*   From $V_{4}$: there are arrows pointing to $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$. $\to (4, 1)$ and $(4, 2)$

steps.title: Map edges to matrix
steps.content:
Now we translate these pairs into '1's in a $4 \times 4$ zero matrix. If there is an edge from $V_{i}$ to $V_{j}$, we place a 1 in row $i$, column $j$.

$$\begin{aligned} (1, 2) &\implies \text{Row 1, Col 2} = 1 \\ (2, 1) &\implies \text{Row 2, Col 1} = 1 \\ (2, 3) &\implies \text{Row 2, Col 3} = 1 \\ (4, 1) &\implies \text{Row 4, Col 1} = 1 \\ (4, 2) &\implies \text{Row 4, Col 2} = 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Construct final matrix
steps.content:
Filling in the rest of the positions with 0, we get our final adjacency matrix:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This follows the standard IB format for representing directed graphs. You would earn 1 mark for correctly identifying the edges and 1 mark for the final matrix structure.

parts.content: Are there any cycles starting at C? Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme: - No, as you cannot reach any other point from C <marks> A1 </marks>

steps.title: Identify the requirement
steps.content:
To determine if a cycle can start at vertex $C$, we need to check if there is a path that begins at vertex $C$, moves through other vertices (or the same one), and eventually returns to $C$.

In a directed graph, any path or cycle must follow the direction of the arrows (edges). Therefore, the first step is to look for any edges pointing **away** from vertex $C$.

steps.title: Inspect vertex C
steps.content:
By visually inspecting the graph, we look at the arrows connected to vertex $C$:

*   There is one arrow pointing from vertex $E$ to vertex $C$.
*   There are **no arrows** pointing away from $C$ toward any other vertex.

In graph theory terms, the **out-degree** of vertex $C$ is 0.

steps.title: Evaluate cycle possibility
steps.content:
Since there are no outgoing edges from $C$, it is impossible to move from $C$ to any other vertex in the graph. 

Because you cannot even take the first step of a path starting at $C$, you certainly cannot follow a sequence of edges that returns to $C$. Thus, no cycle can start at this vertex.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: No, there are no cycles starting at $C$ because you cannot reach any other vertex from vertex $C$.

highlights.text: No, as you cannot reach any other point from C

parts.content: Find the value of $x$, given that the graph has 6 edges.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize the Handshaking Lemma: $\text{sum of degrees} = 2 \times \text{number of edges}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Set up and solve the equation: $2 + 3 + 3 + 1 + x = 2 \times 6 \Rightarrow x = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: Our goal is to find the missing degree $x$ for a graph with 5 vertices. We are given the degrees of the vertices and the total number of edges. This topic is covered in **AHL Topic 3.14—Graph theory**.

steps.title: Recall the Handshaking Lemma
steps.content:
The **Handshaking Lemma** is a fundamental rule in graph theory. It states that for any undirected graph, the sum of the degrees of all vertices is equal to twice the number of edges ($e$):

$$\sum \text{degrees} = 2e$$

This happens because every edge connects two vertices, contributing exactly 1 to the degree count of each endpoint.

steps.title: Calculate the degree sum
steps.content:
First, let's find the sum of the degrees provided in the question. We add the known degrees and the unknown variable $x$:

$$\text{Sum} = 2 + 3 + 3 + 1 + x = 9 + x$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sum in the Run-Matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the sum in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Sum the known vertex degrees.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Now, we use the fact that the graph has 6 edges. According to the Lemma, the sum of degrees must equal $2 \times 6$:

$$\begin{aligned} 9 + x &= 2 \times 6 \\ 9 + x &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the number of edges by 2.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the number of edges by 2.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the number of edges by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate twice the number of edges.

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Finally, solve for $x$ by subtracting 9 from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 12 - 9 \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, recognizing the Lemma earns you the first mark (**M1**), and solving correctly for $x=3$ earns the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Set up the graph
steps.content:
To solve this visually, we start by drawing $5$ vertices (points). Let's label the vertices $A, B, C, D$, and $E$. 

We know that four of these vertices must have the degrees: $2, 3, 3,$ and $1$. We will designate vertex $E$ as the one with degree $x$. Our goal is to draw exactly $6$ edges (lines) such that the degrees of $A, B, C,$ and $D$ match the given values.

steps.title: Distribute the edges
steps.content:
Let's draw $6$ edges to satisfy the requirements for vertices $A, B, C,$ and $D$:

1.  **Vertex D (degree 1):** Connect $D$ to $E$. ($1$ edge used)
2.  **Vertex A (degree 2):** Connect $A$ to $B$ and $A$ to $C$. ($3$ edges used total)
3.  **Vertex B (degree 3):** We already have one edge ($A-B$). Let's add two more: $B$ to $C$ and $B$ to $E$. ($5$ edges used total)
4.  **Vertex C (degree 3):** We already have two edges, $A-C$ and $B-C$. We need one more to make it degree $3$. Let's connect $C$ to $E$. ($6$ edges used total)

Now we have used all $6$ edges. Let's check our degrees:
- $\text{deg}(A) = 2$ (connected to $B, C$)
- $\text{deg}(B) = 3$ (connected to $A, C, E$)
- $\text{deg}(C) = 3$ (connected to $A, B, E$)
- $\text{deg}(D) = 1$ (connected to $E$)

steps.title: Determine degree of x
steps.content:
Now we simply look at our remaining vertex, $E$ (which represents $x$), and count how many edges are connected to it. 

From our construction in the previous step, vertex $E$ is connected to:
- Vertex $D$
- Vertex $B$
- Vertex $C$

Counting these up, we see that vertex $E$ has $3$ edges connected to it.

$$\text{deg}(E) = x = 3$$

steps.title: Verify with Handshaking Lemma
steps.content:
In Graph Theory, the **Handshaking Lemma** states that the sum of the degrees of all vertices is equal to twice the number of edges. This is a great way to double-check our visual result.

$$\text{Sum of degrees} = 2 \times \text{number of edges}$$

If we plug in our values:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 + 3 + 3 + 1 + 3 &= 12 \\ 2 \times 6 &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

Since both sides are equal, our visual construction of $x = 3$ is correct.

parts.content: Use **Prim's Algorithm** to find the minimum spanning tree for the graph starting from vertex $A$. State the edges included in the tree and calculate the total weight of the tree.
parts.markscheme:
- Start at vertex $A$ and identify the edge with the smallest weight, $AD$ (weight 2) <marks>M1</marks>
- Select the next edge with the smallest weight connected to the current tree $\{A, D\}$, which is $AB$ (weight 3) <marks>M1</marks>
- Select the next edge with the smallest weight connected to the current tree $\{A, D, B\}$, which is $BC$ (weight 1) <marks>M1</marks>
- State the edges included ($AD, AB, BC$) and determine the total weight: $2 + 3 + 1 = 6$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Start at vertex A
steps.content:
To find the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using the **Direct Graphical Approach** (Prim's Algorithm), we begin at the specified starting vertex and grow our tree one edge at a time.

Starting at vertex $A$, we look at all the edges connected to it:
- Edge $AB$ with weight $3$
- Edge $AC$ with weight $4$
- Edge $AD$ with weight $2$

We select the edge with the lowest weight, which is $AD$. Our tree now includes vertices $\{A, D\}$.

steps.title: Add the next vertex
steps.content:
Now we look at all edges connected to our current tree $\{A, D\}$ that lead to a vertex not yet included ($B$ or $C$):
- From $A$: $AB$ (weight $3$) and $AC$ (weight $4$)
- From $D$: $DB$ (weight $5$) and $DC$ (weight $6$)

The smallest available weight is $3$ for the edge $AB$. We add edge $AB$ to our tree. 

Our tree now includes vertices $\{A, D, B\}$.

steps.title: Include the final vertex
steps.content:
We need to include the final vertex, $C$. We look at all edges connected to our current tree $\{A, D, B\}$ that lead to $C$:
- From $A$: $AC$ (weight $4$)
- From $D$: $DC$ (weight $6$)
- From $B$: $BC$ (weight $1$)

The edge with the lowest weight is $BC$ with a weight of $1$. We add edge $BC$ to our tree.

All vertices $\{A, B, C, D\}$ are now connected.

steps.title: Calculate the total weight
steps.content:
The edges included in our Minimum Spanning Tree are $AD$, $AB$, and $BC$. 

To find the total weight, we sum the weights of these edges:

$$\text{Total Weight} = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6$$

steps.title: Construct the Weight Matrix
steps.content:
To use the **Tabular Matrix Method**, we first represent the graph as a weight matrix. Each cell represents the weight of the edge between two vertices. If no direct edge exists, we leave it blank or use $\infty$.

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc} & A & B & C & D \\ \hline A & - & 3 & - & 2 \\ B & 3 & - & 1 & 4 \\ C & - & 1 & - & 5 \\ D & 2 & 4 & 5 & - \end{array}$$

steps.title: Start at Vertex A
steps.content:
The question specifies starting at vertex $A$. We follow these steps:
1. Label row $A$ with a '1' to show it is the first vertex in our tree.
2. **Cross out column A** so we don't pick it again.
3. Look at the values in row $A$ that are not crossed out.

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc} & \cancel{A} & B & C & D \\ \hline \text{1: } A & - & 3 & - & 2 \\ B & 3 & - & 1 & 4 \\ C & - & 1 & - & 5 \\ D & 2 & 4 & 5 & - \end{array}$$

In row $A$, the smallest available weight is **2** (the edge to vertex $D$).

steps.title: Add Vertex D
steps.content:
Now we add vertex $D$ to our tree:
1. Label row $D$ with a '2'.
2. **Cross out column D**.
3. Look for the smallest value in **either** row $A$ or row $D$ that is not crossed out.

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc} & \cancel{A} & B & C & \cancel{D} \\ \hline \text{1: } A & - & 3 & - & \cancel{2} \\ B & 3 & - & 1 & \cancel{4} \\ C & - & 1 & - & \cancel{5} \\ \text{2: } D & 2 & 4 & 5 & - \end{array}$$

Available values: $B(3)$ from row $A$, and $B(4), C(5)$ from row $D$. The smallest is **3** (the edge $AB$).

steps.title: Add Vertex B
steps.content:
Next, we add vertex $B$:
1. Label row $B$ with a '3'.
2. **Cross out column B**.
3. Look for the smallest value in rows $A, D$, or $B$ that is not crossed out.

$$\begin{array}{c|cccc} & \cancel{A} & \cancel{B} & C & \cancel{D} \\ \hline \text{1: } A & - & \cancel{3} & - & \cancel{2} \\ \text{3: } B & 3 & - & 1 & \cancel{4} \\ C & - & \cancel{1} & - & \cancel{5} \\ \text{2: } D & 2 & \cancel{4} & 5 & - \end{array}$$

Available values: $C(1)$ from row $B$ and $C(5)$ from row $D$. The smallest is **1** (the edge $BC$).

steps.title: Finalize the Tree
steps.content:
Finally, we add vertex $C$ and cross out column $C$. All vertices are now included in the minimum spanning tree.

**Included Edges:**
1. $AD$ (weight 2)
2. $AB$ (weight 3)
3. $BC$ (weight 1)

Now, we calculate the total weight of the tree.

parts.content: Can this graph be a tree? Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:

- Since $8 - 1 = 7 \neq 10$, conclude that the graph cannot be a tree. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the tree property
steps.content:
To determine if a graph is a tree, we use the fundamental **Edge-Vertex Property**. This property states that for any tree with $n$ vertices, the number of edges $e$ must be exactly $n - 1$.

This is a key concept in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (Graph Theory)**.

steps.title: Apply the vertex count
steps.content:
The problem states that the graph has **8 vertices**. Let's calculate how many edges a tree with this many vertices is required to have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 8 \\
e &= n - 1 \\
e &= 8 - 1 \\
e &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the subtraction in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, enter the subtraction.

desmos.instructions: Type the subtraction into an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the required number of edges for a tree with 8 vertices.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
We are given that the graph has **10 edges**.

Comparing our calculated value to the given value:
$$10 \neq 7$$

Since the number of edges ($10$) is not equal to $n - 1$ ($7$), the graph does not satisfy the necessary property of a tree. Therefore, the graph **cannot** be a tree.

In IB exams, you earn **1 mark** for stating the $n-1$ rule and **1 mark** for the correct comparison and conclusion.

steps.title: Identify graph properties
steps.content:
From the question, we are given a connected graph with:
- Number of vertices ($v$) = 8
- Number of edges ($e$) = 10

steps.title: Define a tree
steps.content:
In **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (Graph Theory)**, we learn that a **tree** is defined as a connected graph that contains **no cycles**. 

A fundamental property of any tree with $v$ vertices is that it must have exactly $e = v - 1$ edges.

steps.title: Calculate required edges
steps.content:
If this graph were a tree with 8 vertices, we can calculate how many edges it should have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= v - 1 \\
e &= 8 - 1 \\
e &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

A tree with 8 vertices must have exactly 7 edges.

gdc.instructions: Enter 8 - 1.

ti84.instructions: Enter 8 - 1 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 8 - 1.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of edges a tree with 8 vertices must have.

steps.title: Analyze cycle existence
steps.content:
We are told the graph has 10 edges.

In graph theory, if a connected graph has a number of edges $e$ such that $e \geq v$ (meaning the number of edges is greater than or equal to the number of vertices), the graph **must contain at least one cycle**.

Since $10 \geq 8$, this graph is guaranteed to have at least one cycle.

steps.title: Formulate conclusion
steps.content:
Because the graph contains at least one cycle, it contradicts the definition of a tree (which must have no cycles).

Therefore, this graph **cannot be a tree**.

parts.content: How many cycles must it contain at minimum?
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the given values into the formula for the minimum number of cycles $e - v + 1$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $10 - 8 + 1 = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
From the question, we are given a connected graph with the following properties:

*   Number of vertices ($V$) = $8$
*   Number of edges ($E$) = $10$

steps.title: Introduce the cycle formula
steps.content:
For a connected graph, the minimum number of fundamental cycles is determined by the **circuit rank** (also known as the cyclomatic number). This formula represents how many edges exist beyond what is needed to form a spanning tree.

The formula is:

$$ \text{Minimum Cycles} = E - V + 1 $$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values $E = 10$ and $V = 8$ into the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Cycles} &= 10 - 8 + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of cycles using the circuit rank formula.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The graph must contain at minimum **3** cycles.

In an IB exam, you would earn **1 mark** for correctly substituting into the formula ($10 - 8 + 1$) and **1 mark** for the correct final answer ($3$).

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the spanning tree method, we first identify the number of vertices ($V$) and edges ($E$) from the question.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 8 \\
E &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Understand spanning tree edges
steps.content:
In graph theory, a **spanning tree** is a connected subgraph that includes all vertices of the original graph but has no cycles. 

For any connected graph with $V$ vertices, a spanning tree must contain exactly $V - 1$ edges.

steps.title: Calculate spanning tree edges
steps.content:
Let's calculate how many edges our graph would need if it were just a tree (meaning it had zero cycles):

$$ \text{Edges in spanning tree} = V - 1 $$

Substituting our value:

$$ 8 - 1 = 7 $$

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction on the main screen.

steps.title: Find the 'extra' edges
steps.content:
The graph actually has $10$ edges. Every edge added to a spanning tree creates exactly one cycle. Therefore, the number of "extra" edges tells us the minimum number of cycles in the graph.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Cycles} &= E - (V - 1) \\
&= 10 - 7 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that this is equivalent to the formula $E - V + 1$.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The connected graph must contain at minimum **3** cycles.

parts.content: Create an adjacency table for the graph.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The goal is to create an **adjacency table** for the given weighted graph. We will use a systematic approach, examining each vertex from $A$ to $E$ one by one and recording the weight of the edges connecting them to every other vertex. If no self-loops exist, we mark the diagonal with a dash ($-$) or $0$.

highlights.text: Create an adjacency table for the graph.

steps.title: Analyze Vertex A
steps.content:
Looking at vertex $A$, we identify its connections and their corresponding weights:
- $A$ to $B$: weight is $3$
- $A$ to $C$: weight is $5$
- $A$ to $D$: weight is $2$
- $A$ to $E$: weight is $4$

This gives us the first row: 

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline A & - & 3 & 5 & 2 & 4 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

steps.title: Analyze Vertex B
steps.content:
Now, we look at vertex $B$ and find all edges extending from it:

- $B$ to $A$: weight is $3$
- $B$ to $C$: weight is $3$
- $B$ to $D$: weight is $2$
- $B$ to $E$: weight is $6$

Notice that the table is symmetric; the value for $B$ to $A$ is the same as $A$ to $B$.

steps.title: Analyze Vertex C
steps.content:
Next, examine vertex $C$:
- $C$ to $A$: weight is $5$
- $C$ to $B$: weight is $3$
- $C$ to $D$: weight is $6$
- $C$ to $E$: weight is $4$

Adding this to our table completes the third row.

steps.title: Analyze Vertex D
steps.content:
Now, examine vertex $D$:
- $D$ to $A$: weight is $2$
- $D$ to $B$: weight is $2$
- $D$ to $C$: weight is $6$
- $D$ to $E$: weight is $5$

steps.title: Analyze Vertex E
steps.content:
Finally, examine vertex $E$:
- $E$ to $A$: weight is $4$
- $E$ to $B$: weight is $6$
- $E$ to $C$: weight is $4$
- $E$ to $D$: weight is $5$

This completes the systematic scan of the graph.

steps.title: Construct the final table
steps.content:
Combining all the rows into the final adjacency table:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline A & - & 3 & 5 & 2 & 4 \\ \hline B & 3 & - & 3 & 2 & 6 \\ \hline C & 5 & 3 & - & 6 & 4 \\ \hline D & 2 & 2 & 6 & - & 5 \\ \hline E & 4 & 6 & 4 & 5 & - \\ \hline \end{array}$$

This table represents all connections and their weights as seen in the graph. In an IB exam, you earn **2 marks** for accurately transferring all weights into the table format.

steps.title: Set up the table
steps.content:
To create an adjacency table for a weighted graph, we first set up a grid with the vertices ($A, B, C, D, E$) as both rows and columns. 

Since this is an undirected graph, our table will be **symmetric** across the diagonal. Also, because there are no loops (edges connecting a vertex to itself), we fill the diagonal cells with a dash ($-$).

steps.title: Record edges from Vertex A
steps.content:
Using the **Edge-by-Edge Symmetric Approach**, we identify all edges connected to vertex $A$ and immediately record them in both symmetric positions:

*   **A to B**: weight $3$. Enter $3$ at $(A, B)$ and $(B, A)$.
*   **A to C**: weight $5$. Enter $5$ at $(A, C)$ and $(C, A)$.
*   **A to D**: weight $2$. Enter $2$ at $(A, D)$ and $(D, A)$.
*   **A to E**: weight $4$. Enter $4$ at $(A, E)$ and $(E, A)$.

steps.title: Record unique edges from B
steps.content:
Next, we look for edges connected to vertex $B$ that haven't been recorded yet:

*   **B to C**: weight $3$. Enter $3$ at $(B, C)$ and $(C, B)$.
*   **B to D**: weight $2$. Enter $2$ at $(B, D)$ and $(D, B)$.
*   **B to E**: weight $6$. Enter $6$ at $(B, E)$ and $(E, B)$.

steps.title: Record unique edges from C
steps.content:
Now we find the remaining unique edges connected to vertex $C$:

*   **C to D**: weight $6$. Enter $6$ at $(C, D)$ and $(D, C)$.
*   **C to E**: weight $4$. Enter $4$ at $(C, E)$ and $(E, C)$.

steps.title: Record the final edge
steps.content:
Finally, we identify the last edge in the graph:

*   **D to E**: weight $5$. Enter $5$ at $(D, E)$ and $(E, D)$.

Double-check that every edge in the diagram is accounted for and that the table is perfectly mirrored across the diagonal.

steps.title: Final Adjacency Table
steps.content:
The completed table matches the markscheme exactly:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline A & - & 3 & 5 & 2 & 4 \\ \hline B & 3 & - & 3 & 2 & 6 \\ \hline C & 5 & 3 & - & 6 & 4 \\ \hline D & 2 & 2 & 6 & - & 5 \\ \hline E & 4 & 6 & 4 & 5 & - \\ \hline \end{array}$$

steps.title: Start at Vertex A
steps.content:
To begin Prim's algorithm using the **Visual Edge Selection** approach, we start at the specified vertex, which is **A**. 

We look at all edges connected to vertex **A** and identify the one with the smallest weight.

steps.title: Select the first edge
steps.content:
From vertex **A**, we have the following connections:
- $AD$ with weight $2$
- $AE$ with weight $4$
- $AB$ with weight $5$
- $AC$ with weight $6$

The shortest edge is **AD** with a weight of **2**. We add this to our tree.

Our current set of visited vertices is $\{A, D\}$. We now look for the shortest edge connecting either **A** or **D** to an unvisited vertex ($B, C, or E$):
- From $A$: $AE (4), AB (5), AC (6)$
- From $D$: $DB (2), DC (4), DE (9)$

The shortest available edge is **DB** (or $BD$) with a weight of **2**. We add vertex **B** to our tree.

steps.title: Select the third edge
steps.content:
Our visited set is now $\{A, D, B\}$. We look for the shortest edge connecting any of these to unvisited vertices ($C or E$):
- From $B$: $BC (3), BE (7)$
- From $D$: $DC (4)$
- From $A$: $AE (4), AC (6)$

The shortest available edge is **BC** with a weight of **3**. We add vertex **C** to our tree.

Our visited set is $\{A, D, B, C\}$. Only vertex **E** remains unvisited. We check all edges from our visited set to **E**:
- $AE (4)$
- $BE (7)$
- $CE (8)$
- $DE (9)$

The shortest edge to reach **E** is **AE** with a weight of **4**. Our Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is now complete.

To find the total weight of the Minimum Spanning Tree, we sum the weights of the edges we selected:

$$ \text{Total Weight} = 2 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 11 $$

steps.title: Construct the Weight Matrix
steps.content:
To find the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using the tabular approach, we first represent the graph as a weight matrix. Each row and column represents a vertex ($A, B, C, D, E$). The entries in the matrix are the weights of the edges between them. If no edge exists, we use $\infty$.

$$\begin{pmatrix} & A & B & C & D & E \\ A & - & 4 & \infty & 2 & 4 \\ B & 4 & - & 3 & 2 & 6 \\ C & \infty & 3 & - & 4 & 7 \\ D & 2 & 2 & 4 & - & 5 \\ E & 4 & 6 & 7 & 5 & - \end{pmatrix}$$

This table allows us to systematically track the shortest distance from our growing tree to the remaining vertices.

steps.title: Select the Starting Vertex
steps.content:
We start at vertex **A**. 

1. Label column **A** with a **1** to show it's the first vertex in our tree.
2. **Cross out row A** because we are looking for edges leading *out* of the tree to new vertices.

Now, look at the available weights in the column(s) of the vertices already in the tree (currently just column A):
- To B: 4

Since the edge $(A, D)$ is the shortest, we add vertex **D** to our MST.

1. Label column **D** with a **2**.
2. **Cross out row D**.

Now we look at the minimum values in both columns **A** and **D** for the remaining vertices ($B, C, E$):
- **B**: Look at row B, columns A and D. Values are 4 and 2. Min is **2** (from D).
- **C**: Look at row C, columns A and D. Values are $\infty$ and 4. Min is **4** (from D).
- **E**: Look at row E, columns A and D. Values are 4 and 5. Min is **4** (from A).

The smallest overall value is **2**, connecting **D to B**.

steps.title: Add Vertex B
steps.content:
Next, we add vertex **B** to the tree.

1. Label column **B** with a **3**.
2. **Cross out row B**.

Compare the minimum distances from the current tree ($A, D, B$) to the remaining vertices ($C, E$):
- **C**: Columns A, D, B have values $\infty, 4, 3$. Min is **3** (from B).
- **E**: Columns A, D, B have values $4, 5, 6$. Min is **4** (from A).

The smallest weight is **3**, connecting **B to C**.

steps.title: Add Vertex C
steps.content:
Now we add vertex **C** to the tree.

1. Label column **C** with a **4**.
2. **Cross out row C**.

Finally, we look for the shortest connection to the last vertex, **E**:
- **E**: Columns A, D, B, C have values $4, 5, 6, 7$. 

The minimum weight is **4**, which connects **A to E**.

steps.title: Calculate Total MST Weight
steps.content:
All vertices are now included in the tree. The edges selected for the Minimum Spanning Tree are:

- $A$ to $D$ (weight $2$)
- $D$ to $B$ (weight $2$)
- $B$ to $C$ (weight $3$)

We sum these weights to find the total length of the MST:

steps.title: Understand Kruskal's Algorithm
steps.content:
Kruskal's algorithm is used to find the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a graph. The goal is to connect all vertices with the minimum total edge weight without forming any cycles. 

Using the **Tabular Sorting Method**, we will list every edge in the graph, sort them from smallest to largest weight, and then systematically decide whether to include them in our tree.

steps.title: Sort Edges by Weight
steps.content:
First, we list all the edges present in the graph and sort them in ascending order of their weights:

| Edge | Weight |
| :--- | :--- |
| AD | 2 |
| BD | 2 |
| BC | 3 |
| AE | 4 |
| AB | 5 |
| CD | 6 |
| DE | 7 |
| CE | 8 |

Since the graph has $n = 5$ vertices (A, B, C, D, and E), our MST must contain exactly $n - 1 = 4$ edges.

steps.title: Apply Tabular Selection
steps.content:
We now go through our sorted list and select edges as long as they do not create a cycle (a closed loop).

| Edge | Weight | Decision | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AD** | **2** | **Accept** | First edge selected. |
| **BD** | **2** | **Accept** | Connects B to the tree; no cycle formed. |
| **BC** | **3** | **Accept** | Connects C to the tree; no cycle formed. |
| **AE** | **4** | **Accept** | Connects E to the tree; no cycle formed. |

We have now selected 4 edges, which is $n-1$. The algorithm terminates here.

steps.title: Calculate Total Weight
steps.content:
To find the total weight of the Minimum Spanning Tree, we sum the weights of the accepted edges:

$$ \text{Total Weight} = 2 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 11 $$

steps.title: Understand Kruskal's Algorithm
steps.content:
Kruskal’s algorithm is an **Incremental Selection Method** used to find the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). 

The goal is to connect all vertices using the minimum total weight without creating any cycles (closed loops). We do this by listing all edges from the smallest weight to the largest and selecting them one by one, skipping any that would form a cycle.

steps.title: Sort edges by weight
steps.content:
First, we identify all the edges in the graph and sort them in ascending order of weight:

*   **AD**: $2$
*   **BD**: $2$
*   **BC**: $3$
*   **AE**: $4$
*   **CD**: $4$
*   **AB**: $5$
*   **DE**: $6$
*   **CE**: $7$

This sorted list tells us the order in which we should consider adding edges.

steps.title: Select shortest edges
steps.content:
Following the Incremental Selection Method, we start with the shortest available edges:

1.  **Select AD (weight 2)**: This is the shortest edge. We add it to our MST.
2.  **Select BD (weight 2)**: The next shortest. Adding this does not create a cycle, so we include it.

Our MST currently consists of vertices {A, B, D} connected by weight $2 + 2 = 4$.

steps.title: Add next available edge
steps.content:
The next shortest edge in our list is **BC (weight 3)**. 

Adding edge BC connects vertex C to our tree. Since there is no closed loop formed, we accept this edge.

Currently, we have connected vertices {A, B, C, D}.

steps.title: Check for cycles
steps.content:
We still need to connect vertex E. The next edges on our list both have a weight of $4$: **AE** and **CD**.

*   **Consider CD (4)**: If we added CD, we would form a cycle (B-C-D-B). Therefore, we **reject** edge CD.
*   **Consider AE (4)**: Adding AE connects the final vertex (E) to the rest of the graph without creating a cycle. We **accept** edge AE.

Now all 5 vertices are connected using 4 edges, which completes the tree.

steps.title: Calculate total weight
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the weights of the selected edges to find the total weight of the Minimum Spanning Tree:

$$ \text{Total Weight} = 2 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 11 $$

steps.title: Analyze the graph properties
steps.content: The question specifies that $G$ is a **connected** graph with **eight vertices**. We want to find the smallest number of edges possible that can still keep all 8 vertices connected in a single component.

highlights.text: G is a simple, connected graph with eight vertices.

steps.title: Identify the minimal structure
steps.content:
In graph theory, a connected graph with the minimum possible number of edges is called a **tree**. 

If we had fewer edges, the graph would fall apart into multiple pieces (it wouldn't be connected). If we added more edges, we would create cycles, meaning we'd have more edges than strictly necessary for connectivity.

steps.title: Apply the tree formula
steps.content:
A fundamental property for any tree is the relationship between the number of vertices ($v$) and the number of edges ($e$):

$$e = v - 1$$

This relationship is a core concept within **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (Graph Theory)**.

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Since we know the graph has 8 vertices, we substitute $v = 8$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} e &= 8 - 1 \\ e &= 7 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the minimum number of edges is 7.

steps.title: Visualize the starting state
steps.content: To find the minimum number of edges, let's start by visualizing the eight vertices of graph $G$ as isolated points on a page. At this stage, with zero edges, we have 8 separate components (islands).

steps.title: Connect vertices step-by-step
steps.content:
To make the graph **connected**, we need to link these vertices using the fewest edges possible. Each time we add an edge to connect a new vertex to our existing group, the number of separate components decreases by exactly one:

*   **Edge 1:** Connects 2 vertices (7 components remain).
*   **Edge 2:** Connects a 3rd vertex to the group (6 components remain).
*   **Edge 3:** Connects a 4th vertex (5 components remain).

If we continue this pattern, we are building what is known in graph theory as a **tree**.

steps.title: Determine the minimum edges
steps.content:
Following the inductive process, to reduce the number of components from $n$ down to $1$, we always need $n - 1$ edges. 

For a graph with $v = 8$ vertices:
$$\text{Minimum edges} = 8 - 1 = 7$$

Any fewer than 7 edges would leave at least two vertices disconnected from each other.

steps.title: Analyze the given information
steps.content:
In graph theory, specifically for planar graphs, we are looking at the relationship between edges and faces. The question provides two key pieces of information:
1. There are $f$ faces in the graph $H$.
2. Every single face is bounded by exactly $k$ edges.

This question is part of **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (AHL 3.14 Graph theory)**. Understanding how edges and faces interact is essential for working with Euler's formula for planar graphs.

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges.

steps.title: Count edges per face
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the total number of boundary edges by looking at each face individually. If one face has $k$ edges, and there are $f$ faces in total, the sum of all edges counted face-by-face is:

$$\text{Total boundary count} = k \times f$$

highlights.text: counting the edges around every face gives kf edges

steps.title: Apply the 'Handshaking' principle
steps.content:
Now, we need to consider how many times each edge was counted in that sum. In a planar graph, every edge serves as a boundary for exactly two faces (the two regions it separates). 

Because every edge $e$ is shared by exactly two faces, it has been counted twice in our previous calculation ($kf$).

highlights.text: every edge is counted in exactly 2 faces

steps.title: Equate and conclude
steps.content:
Since our count of $kf$ represents every edge being counted exactly twice, we can write the relationship as:

$$kf = 2e$$

Rearranging this gives us the required explanation:

$$2e = kf$$

steps.title: Define face degree
steps.content: In a planar graph, the **degree of a face** is defined as the number of edges that form its boundary. The question tells us that every face in graph $H$ is bounded by exactly $k$ edges, so the degree of every face is $k$.

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges

steps.title: Sum the face degrees
steps.content:
Since there are $f$ faces and each face has a degree of $k$, we can find the sum of all face degrees by multiplying these two values:

$$\sum \text{deg}(f_i) = k \times f = kf$$

steps.title: Apply the Handshaking Lemma
steps.content:
A fundamental theorem in graph theory (often called the Handshaking Lemma for faces) states that the sum of the degrees of all faces in a planar graph is equal to twice the number of edges ($2e$):

$$\sum \text{deg}(f_i) = 2e$$

This is because in a planar graph, every edge acts as a boundary for exactly **two faces**. Therefore, when we count the edges around every face, each edge is counted exactly twice.

steps.title: Conclude the result
steps.content:
By substituting our sum from Step 1 into the theorem from Step 2, we arrive at the required equation:

$$kf = 2e$$

steps.title: Construct the dual graph $H^*$
steps.content:
To explain this relationship using the dual graph approach, we first consider the dual graph $H^*$. In this graph, every face of the original graph $H$ is represented as a vertex. 

Since $H$ has $f$ faces, our dual graph $H^*$ will have exactly $f$ vertices.

steps.title: Determine the vertex degrees
steps.content:
The question tells us that every face in $H$ is bounded by exactly $k$ edges. In graph theory, the degree of a vertex in the dual graph $H^*$ corresponds to the number of edges bounding the face it represents in $H$.

Because every face has $k$ boundaries, every vertex in $H^*$ must have a degree of $k$.

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges.

steps.title: Apply the Handshaking Lemma
steps.content:
Now we apply the **Handshaking Lemma** (from **Topic 3.14: Graph theory**) to the dual graph $H^*$. This lemma states that the sum of the degrees of all vertices in a graph is equal to twice the number of edges ($2e$):

$$\sum \text{deg}(v) = 2e$$

In our dual graph $H^*$:
1. There are $f$ vertices.
2. Each vertex has a degree of $k$.

So, the sum of the degrees is:
$$\underbrace{k + k + \dots + k}_{f \text{ times}} = kf$$

Setting the sum of degrees equal to $2e$, we get:
$$kf = 2e$$

steps.title: Identify Euler's Formula
steps.content:
To solve problems involving the vertices, edges, and faces of a connected planar graph, we use **Euler's Formula**. This formula relates these three properties in a very simple way:

$$v - e + f = 2$$

From the question, we are given that the number of vertices is 9.

steps.title: Use the face-edge relationship
steps.content:
Next, we need a second equation to relate the edges ($e$) and faces ($f$). The question states that every face is bounded by exactly $k$ edges, where $k=3$. 

In graph theory, if every face is bounded by $k$ edges, then the sum of all edges around all faces is $k \times f$. Since each edge in a planar graph is shared by exactly two faces, we have the relationship:

$$2e = kf$$

Substituting $k = 3$:

$$2e = 3f$$

steps.title: Express e in terms of f
steps.content:
To use the algebraic substitution method, we isolate $e$ in our second equation so we can substitute it into Euler's formula.

$$\begin{aligned} 2e &= 3f \\ e &= \frac{3f}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve for f
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $v = 9$ and $e = \frac{3f}{2}$ into Euler's formula ($v - e + f = 2$):

$$9 - \frac{3f}{2} + f = 2$$

To make this easier to solve, we can combine the $f$ terms. Note that $f$ is the same as $\frac{2f}{2}$:

$$9 - \frac{3f}{2} + \frac{2f}{2} = 2$$

$$9 - \frac{1}{2}f = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now we finish the algebra to find the value of $f$:

$$\begin{aligned} 9 - 2 &= \frac{1}{2}f \\ 7 &= \frac{1}{2}f \\ f &= 14 \end{aligned}$$

So, the graph has 14 faces.

steps.title: Identify the relevant variables
steps.content:
Hi! Let's solve this graph theory problem. We are looking for the number of faces, $f$, in a connected planar graph. We are given the number of vertices and the number of edges bounding each face:

- Number of vertices, $v = 9$
- Number of edges bounding each face, $k = 3$

To find $f$, we need to set up a system of equations using **Euler's formula** and the relationship between edges and faces.

steps.title: Formulate the first equation
steps.content:
For any connected planar graph, Euler's formula states:

$$v - e + f = 2$$

Substituting the known value $v = 9$, we get our first equation in terms of $e$ and $f$:

$$9 - e + f = 2$$
$$-e + f = -7$$ (Equation 1)

steps.title: Formulate the second equation
steps.content:
Next, we use the fact that every face is bounded by exactly $k$ edges. In graph theory, the sum of the degrees of the faces is equal to twice the number of edges ($2e$). Since every face has the same number of edges $k$:

$$kf = 2e$$

Substituting $k = 3$:

$$3f = 2e$$
$$-2e + 3f = 0$$ (Equation 2)

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges.

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{cases} -e + f = -7 \\ -2e + 3f = 0 \end{cases}$$

Let's use the elimination method. Multiply Equation 1 by $-2$:

$$2e - 2f = 14$$

Now, add this to Equation 2:

$$\begin{aligned} (2e - 2f) + (-2e + 3f) &= 14 + 0 \\ f &= 14 \end{aligned}$$

We can verify this with a calculator.

steps.title: Identify core relationships
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Algebraic Factorization Method**, we start with two fundamental relationships for a connected, planar graph:

1. **Euler's Formula**: For any connected planar graph, the relationship between vertices ($v$), edges ($e$), and faces ($f$) is:
$$v - e + f = 2$$

2. **The Face-Edge Relationship**: Since every face is bounded by exactly $k$ edges, and each edge is shared by exactly two faces, we have:
$$2e = kf$$

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges

steps.title: Combine the equations
steps.content:
We can eliminate $e$ by rearranging the face-edge relationship to $e = \frac{kf}{2}$ and substituting it into Euler's formula:

$$v - \frac{kf}{2} + f = 2$$

To clear the fraction, multiply the entire equation by 2:

$$2v - kf + 2f = 4$$

Now, let's rearrange this to group the terms with $f$ on one side and the terms with $v$ on the other:

$$\begin{aligned} 2v - 4 &= kf - 2f \\ 2v - 4 &= (k - 2)f \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given value
steps.content:
The question provides the number of vertices, $v = 13$. Let's substitute this into our equation to find the constant product:

$$\begin{aligned} (k - 2)f &= 2(13) - 4 \\ (k - 2)f &= 26 - 4 \\ (k - 2)f &= 22 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify possible values

Since $k$ and $f$ must be integers, the value of $f$ must be a factor of 22. The factors of 22 are 1, 2, 11, and 22. 

In a connected planar graph where faces are bounded by $k$ edges, we must have $k \geq 3$ (the smallest cycle has 3 edges). This means the factor $(k - 2)$ must be at least $1$ ($3 - 2 = 1$).

Additionally, for the faces to be "bounded" by edges, the graph must contain cycles, which implies $f > 1$. 

By testing the factors:
- If $(k-2) = 11$, then $f = 2$ (and $k = 13$)
- If $(k-2) = 2$, then $f = 11$ (and $k = 4$)
- If $(k-2) = 1$, then $f = 22$ (and $k = 3$)

Therefore, the possible values for $f$ are 2, 11, and 22.

steps.title: Identify fundamental graph relationships
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we need to use two fundamental relationships for planar graphs. 

First, we use **Euler's formula** for connected planar graphs:
$$v - e + f = 2$$

Second, we use the relationship between edges ($e$), faces ($f$), and the number of edges bounding each face ($k$). Since every face is bounded by exactly $k$ edges, and each edge is shared by exactly two faces in a planar embedding, we have:
$$2e = kf$$

highlights.text: Every face in H is bounded by exactly k edges

steps.title: Express f in terms of k
steps.content:
We are given that the number of vertices $v = 13$. We can substitute $e = \frac{kf}{2}$ into Euler's formula to create an equation with only $f$ and $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} v - e + f &= 2 \\ 13 - \frac{kf}{2} + f &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

Now, let's rearrange this to solve for $f$:

$$\begin{aligned} 11 &= \frac{kf}{2} - f \\ 11 &= f\left(\frac{k}{2} - 1\right) \\ 11 &= f\left(\frac{k - 2}{2}\right) \\ 22 &= f(k - 2) \\ f &= \frac{22}{k - 2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Test values of k
steps.content:
Using the **Systematic Substitution Method**, we test integer values for $k$. Since a face must be bounded by at least 3 edges in a simple graph, we start at $k = 3$. We are looking for values of $k$ that result in $f$ being an integer.

- If $k = 3$: $f = \frac{22}{3 - 2} = 22$
- If $k = 4$: $f = \frac{22}{4 - 2} = 11$
- If $k = 5$: $f = \frac{22}{5 - 2} \approx 7.33$ (Not an integer)
- If $k = 6$: $f = \frac{22}{6 - 2} = 5.5$ (Not an integer)

We only need to check values of $k-2$ that are factors of 22 (1, 2, 11, 22).

ti84.instructions: Go to Y1 and enter 22/(X-2). Press 2nd -> TABLE to see values for X=3, 4, 5, etc.

steps.title: Identify all valid faces
steps.content:
Continuing our search for factors of 22:

- $k - 2 = 11 \Rightarrow k = 13$: $f = \frac{22}{11} = 2$
- $k - 2 = 22 \Rightarrow k = 24$: $f = \frac{22}{22} = 1$

In graph theory, for a connected planar graph where every face is bounded by $k$ edges, we must have at least one cycle (unless $f=1$, which represents a tree). However, the markscheme specifies $f > 1$ because the problem implies the boundary property applies to multiple faces.

Thus, the possible values for $f$ are $2, 11,$ and $22$.

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content:
To answer this question, we need to understand what makes a graph **connected**. This concept is part of **Topic 3.14: Graph Theory**. 

In graph theory, a graph is considered connected if it is "all in one piece," meaning you can travel from any starting vertex to any other destination vertex along the edges provided.

highlights.text: Determine whether the graph is connected.

steps.title: Apply path definition
steps.content:
Using the **General Path Definition** approach, we check if a path exists between every possible pair of vertices in the graph. 

A **path** is a sequence of edges that joins a sequence of distinct vertices. If we can find a path between every pair of vertices (for example, between $A$ and $E$, or $B$ and $C$), the graph is connected.

steps.title: Verify paths in graph
steps.content:
Let's look at the given graph and test some pairs:

*   To get from vertex $B$ to vertex $C$, we can take the path $B \rightarrow A \rightarrow C$.
*   To get from vertex $D$ to vertex $E$, there is a direct edge $D \rightarrow E$.
*   To get from vertex $B$ to vertex $E$, we can follow $B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$.

Since there are no isolated vertices or separate "islands" of vertices, a path exists between every pair of vertices in this graph.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content:
Based on our observation, the graph is indeed **connected**.

**Justification:** The graph is connected because a path exists between every possible pair of vertices in the graph (for example, a path exists from vertex $B$ to vertex $C$ via vertex $A$).

steps.title: Define connectivity
steps.content: In graph theory, a graph is considered **connected** if there is a path between every pair of vertices. This doesn't mean every vertex must have a direct edge between them (which is called adjacency); it simply means you can travel from any vertex to any other vertex by following one or more edges.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Looking at the provided graph, we can see that no part of the graph is isolated from the rest. Therefore, the graph is connected.

steps.title: Justify with a path
steps.content:
To justify this, we can provide a specific example of a path between two vertices that are not directly adjacent. 

For instance, consider **vertex B** and **vertex C**. Although there is no single edge directly connecting them, we can find a path through **vertex A**:

$$B \rightarrow A \rightarrow C$$

Because we can find a sequence of edges to get from one vertex to another for any pair in the graph, it meets the requirement for connectivity.

steps.title: Define connectivity in graphs
steps.content: In graph theory, a graph is considered **connected** if there is a path between every pair of vertices. Using **Single Component Analysis**, we check if the entire graph consists of one single piece (a component) rather than multiple disconnected sub-graphs or isolated vertices.

steps.title: Analyze the graph components
steps.content:
Let's look at the structure of the provided graph. We can see that all the vertices ($A, B, C, D,$ and $E$) are linked together. 

There are no **isolated vertices** (vertices with no edges) and no parts of the graph that are completely cut off from the rest. Because you can travel from any vertex to any other vertex by following the edges, the graph consists of exactly **one component**.

steps.title: Provide a specific justification
steps.content:
To satisfy the IB markscheme requirements, we should provide an example of a path between two vertices that aren't directly adjacent. 

For instance, even though vertex $B$ and vertex $C$ are not directly connected by a single edge, a path exists between them:
$$B \rightarrow A \rightarrow C$$

Since such paths exist for all pairs, the graph is connected.
